title: Region pinning + evidence request: dedicated tenancy & snapshot purge
preview:
From: Olivia Turner <olivia.turner@acmepay.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Acme Payments Security <security@acmepay.com>
Date: 2026-07-25T15:12:00-07:00
Subject: Region pinning — evidence we need for procurement

Hi Mateo —

We're progressing towards a dedicated deployment with Redwood and procurement needs a concise evidence package on a few items before legal signs off. Can you confirm the following for Acme's tenant (tenant_acme_01)?

1) Region pinning: once we pin to a US region, what safeguards prevent cross-region replication (control plane and data plane)?
2) Snapshots/backups: where are snapshots stored, are they geo-replicated, and what is the retention policy?
3) Deletion/erase proof: if Acme requests a full tenant scrub, can you provide artifacts showing the timeline and the operations performed (snapshots deleted, tombstone records, purge logs)?
4) Disaster backups: in the event of restore from backup, can restores be restricted back into the pinned region?

Procurement asks for any sample artifacts (manifest, purge logs, runbook excerpts) and a short explanation we can hand to our compliance team. Attached is the evidence template procurement prefers — feel free to populate the fields you can. Also, can we set a 30-minute call early next week to walk through?

Thanks,
Olivia
Olivia Turner
Sr. InfoSec Program Manager — Acme Payments
--
Attachment: acme_evidence_template.xlsx (spreadsheet template)


From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.ai>
To: Olivia Turner <olivia.turner@acmepay.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-07-26T09:04:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Region pinning — evidence we need for procurement

Hi Olivia —

Thanks for the clear list. High level answers below; I'll follow up with attachments and an ops-signed runbook snippet. We can absolutely do a 30-minute walk-through — how does Tuesday 7/28 10:00 PT look?

Quick answers (summary):
- Region pinning: Dedicated tenants are pinned via placement policies at provisioning time. Data plane and control plane components are constrained to the selected AZ/region; we do not place scheduled replicas in other regions under the pinned deployment. Network controls + host affinities enforce the boundary.
- Snapshots/backups: Snapshot artifacts live in a region-bound object store by default. Backups are NOT geo-replicated unless explicitly configured as a customer option; default dedicated behavior is single-region snapshot retention to meet locality requirements.
- Deletion/erase proof: We can provide snapshot manifests, purge logs (job ids + timestamps), and the per-tenant tombstone sweep report. For legal/compliance we provide a timeline CSV that shows each delete/expire operation and the ops ticket id.
- Restores: Restores for dedicated tenants are performed into the same pinned region by default; cross-region restore is a distinct opt-in path and requires explicit authorization.

I'll put together the runbook excerpt and the snapshot manifest for tenant_acme_01 and loop ops on this thread to compile purge logs.

Best,
Mateo
Director, Solutions — Redwood Inference


From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-07-26T10:22:00-07:00
Subject: Internal: Acme procurement evidence pack (tenant_acme_01) — action items

Team —

Acme (Procurement + InfoSec) requested artifacts to demonstrate region pinning, backup locality, and a scrub proof for tenant_acme_01. Asked for a short package we can hand to them and a 30-min walkthrough Tue 7/28 10:00 PT. Please prepare the following by EOD Monday:

- Laura: export snapshot-manifest-20260720.csv for tenant_acme_01 (include snapshot IDs, storage location, created_at, size, retention TTL).
- Soojin: pull purge job logs for tenant_acme_01 covering the past 24 months (job ids, timestamps, operator, result). Redact any unrelated tenant ids.
- Ben: draft a 1-page runbook excerpt (pdf) showing the tenant scrub workflow: steps, roles, expected artifacts produced (manifests, tombstone list, S3 delete operations), and the verification checklist. Use redwood-dedicated-wipe-runbook.pdf as the basis.

Attachables to produce: snapshot-manifest-20260720.csv, redwood-dedicated-wipe-runbook.pdf, and a populated acme_evidence_template.xlsx with short free-text answers for each procurement field.

If there are any constraints (e.g., backup copies that are archived off-region for >90 days), flag them immediately so we can disclose.

Thanks — Mateo


From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.ai>
To: Olivia Turner <olivia.turner@acmepay.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-07-28T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Region pinning — evidence we need for procurement

Olivia —

Thanks for the patience. Per our internal ops run, attached are the artifacts you requested for tenant_acme_01:

- snapshot-manifest-20260720.csv — lists snapshot ids, storage bucket (us-west-2), created_at timestamps and retention TTLs. Default snapshot retention for dedicated tenants is 30 days unless a custom retention was requested.
- redwood-dedicated-wipe-runbook.pdf (excerpt) — shows the tenant scrub workflow, operators involved, and verification steps.
- acme_evidence_template.xlsx — we filled the fields with short answers and pointers to the artifacts above.

Short clarifications:
- Region pinning: control plane and data plane placement is enforced by our provisioning layer. We do not spin up scheduled replicas in other regions for a pinned dedicated tenant. Any cross-region replication must be explicitly enabled via a contract amendment.
- Backups/snapshots: for tenant_acme_01, snapshots are stored in a region-bound object store (us-west-2) and are not geo-replicated by default. Archived backups older than 90 days are moved to long-term archive which may be subject to replication depending on the archive tier — we flagged none for tenant_acme_01.
- Deletion proof: attached purge logs include job ids and timestamps (we've redacted internal-only metadata). Restore operations are gated to the pinned region.

Next step: let's take the 30-minute walkthrough Tue 7/28 10:00 PT to go over the artifacts and answer any follow-ups. If that time doesn't work, propose alternatives.

Best regards,
Mateo Alvarez
Director, Solutions — Redwood Inference

Subject: Unexpected mid-month add-on charge on invoice (INV-20260715)

Hi Mateo,

Hope youre well. We just received Julys invoice and theres a $1,800 charge listed as "NLP throughput add-on" that appears to have been applied mid-month. We increased capacity on 2026-07-10, but the invoice shows the full-month add-on amount instead of a pro-rated charge for the remainder of the billing cycle.

Can you review and either (a) issue a revised invoice that prorates the add-on from 2026-07-12 to 2026-07-31, or (b) issue a credit/voucher that offsets the overage? Our procurement team needs this adjusted before we process payment.

Invoice attached: INV-20260715-GREENVALE-ADDON.pdf

Also please confirm VAT handling; Greenvale operates in the US but we have subsidiary billing in the EU for some services.

Thanks,
Amina Yusuf

Greenvale Health

Team,

Forwarding Aminas message from Greenvale (attached). They were charged the full add-on amount despite the increase happening mid-cycle (our change log indicates 2026-07-12). Can billing please: 
- Confirm the correct proration calculation for the add-on from 2026-07-12 to 2026-07-31;
- If an overcharge occurred, issue a credit or voucher that the customer can apply immediately so they can proceed with payment;
- Flag any VAT implications and whether we need Greenvales EU VAT ID to correctly zero-rate or tax the adjustment.

Mateo



From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Amina Yusuf <amina.yusuf@greenvalehealth.com>
Date: Fri, 17 Jul 2026 11:33:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Unexpected mid-month add-on charge on invoice (INV-20260715) -- calculations and proposed remedy

Hi all,

Thanks for the heads-up, Mateo. I pulled the billing records and confirmed:
- Add-on SKU: NLP-THROUGHPUT-M (full month price $1,800)
- Effective activation: 2026-07-12 14:03 UTC (system change_log_20260712.csv)
- Billing cycle: month starting 2026-07-01 to 2026-07-31 (30 days)

Pro-rate calculation we used: prorated_amount = full_price * (days_remaining / days_in_cycle) = $1,800 * (20/30) = $1,200.
The invoice shows $1,800; therefore the overcharge is $600.

Recommended remediation steps:
1) Issue an immediate credit of $600 against INV-20260715 (applied now, visible in the customer portal).
2) Generate a voucher code for $600 (single-use, expires 2026-10-15) so Aminas team can apply to the next invoice if they prefer a voucher instead of a direct credit.
3) Produce a corrected invoice (DRAFT attached: INV-20260718-GREENVALE-REV.pdf) and mark the original as superseded.

VAT note: Greenvale did not provide an EU VAT ID in the original PO. For the credit/voucher we can issue net of VAT if a valid EU VAT ID is supplied; otherwise we will keep the same VAT treatment as the original invoice. Amina: please share the VAT ID if applicable.

Ive applied the credit in the system pending confirmation from the customer; voucher code: RVR-600-GV-07. Laura (Billing Manager)

Attachments: INV-20260718-GREENVALE-REV.pdf

--
Laura Bennett
Billing Manager | Redwood Inference
billing@redwood.ai | +1 888-REDWOOD x430

Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 02 Jul 2026 08:15:00 -0700
Subject: Intro + joint review for ACME pipeline

Hi Mateo (and Vanessa),

Thanks for the quick intro earlier this week. We've been tracking a few ACME opportunities that line up well for a GCP co-sell route and Daniel (GCP PM) asked us to put together a short joint review so we can align prioritization and next steps. High level: two pre-prod pilots and one larger renewal (embedded inference).

Proposed objectives for the call:
- Quick intake on each opportunity (current stage, expected close date)
- Marketplace listing / certification blockers that matter for ACME
- Who needs to own artifacts (SLA, security questionnaire, pricing)

Are you available next week for a 30–45 minute sync? Daniel can join from GCP; I'll loop him once we have a time.

Thanks,
Priya
Priya Menon
GCP Partner Manager
priya.menon@gcp-partners.com

--
(I'm attaching a short summary of the three opportunities in a Slides link — I can send the sheet if helpful.)

From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com>
To: Priya Menon <priya.menon@gcp-partners.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 03 Jul 2026 09:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Intro + joint review for ACME pipeline

Priya — thanks for reaching out. 30–45 minutes works. Suggested agenda and owners below; attaching a joint-opps-sheet.xlsx with account-level notes so we don't re-run intake on the call.

Agenda (30m):
1) Quick intros (3m) — who owns which artifact (Redwood / GCP / ACME)
2) Opportunity triage (12m) — each opp, stage, blocker, ask to GCP/Redwood
3) Marketplace readiness checklist & timeline (8m) — what prevents listing/cert today
4) Action items + owners (7m)

Proposed attendees: Mateo (sales), Vanessa (partner ops), Karthik (solutions), Daniel (GCP PM), Priya (GCP partner). Laura — would you be able to cover the renewal commercial context for ACME?

Proposed times (PDT):

title: Rolling latency incident — proposed tiered remedy
preview:
From: Olivia Grant <olivia@novahealth.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Adler <ben.adler@novahealth.com>
Date: Mon, 28 Jun 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: API latency spikes impacting NovaHealth production

Hi Mateo —

We saw recurring latency spikes across multiple routes this morning and yesterday evening (US‑east region). The spikes caused timeouts on some of our billing-critical jobs; we estimate roughly 15–20% of requests returned 5xx or timed out between 02:10–03:45 UTC and again between 18:20–19:05 UTC on 6/27–6/28.

This is causing downstream billing reconciliation delays and customer-facing errors. We need a remediation plan and a concrete credit/offset proposal while your team investigates root cause. Our contract SLOs reference 99.9% API availability and <250ms p99 latency; the observed behavior is materially below that.

Specific asks:
- A short-term credit to cover the apparent hit to our July spend while you remediate (estimate placeholder: 20% off July invoice)
- A shared incident timeline and any interim mitigations you’ll apply
- Confirmation of whether cached prefix behavior or routing changes could avoid recurrence

We’re happy to coordinate a quick call today if that helps.

Thanks,
Olivia Grant
Head of Platform Reliability
NovaHealth

Attachments: incident_screenshot_1.png (image), request_log_sample.json (attachment stub)


From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com>
To: Olivia Grant <olivia@novahealth.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 28 Jun 2026 11:05:12 -0700
Subject: Re: API latency spikes impacting NovaHealth production

Hi Olivia — thank you for flagging this and sorry for the disruption. I pulled the prelim signals and have looped in Karthik and Marissa to get ops and commercial aligned.

Short answer: we will make this right. Proposed immediate steps:
1) Ops will share an incident timeline and a short remediation plan by EOD.
2) Commercial (Marissa) and I will propose a temporary offset structure to cover the impacted window while we complete root cause analysis.

Early proposal (subject to ops validation): a tiered retroactive offset that scales with measured impact:
- <10% of requests affected in the billing month: 5% invoice credit
- 10–20% affected: 20% invoice credit for July
- >20% affected: 40% invoice credit plus a one‑month capacity voucher (value capped)

We’ll cap total vendor exposure at $75k for a single incident and provide precise impact calculations once Karthik confirms the numbers. Also attaching a short incident_timeline.csv and a preliminary SLO export we pulled (slo_report.pdf) for your review.

If that framework looks reasonable to you, I’ll request executive sign‑off today and get a DocuSign amendment prepped for the credit.

— Mateo
Mateo Alvarez
Senior Account Executive, Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference

Attachments: incident_timeline.csv (csv), slo_report.pdf (pdf)


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Olivia Grant <olivia@novahealth.com>, Ben Adler <ben.adler@novahealth.com>
Date: Tue, 29 Jun 2026 08:30:45 -0700
Subject: Re: API latency spikes impacting NovaHealth production

Folks — ops update and recommended remedy structure below.

Summary of root cause (prelim): routing instability introduced by a rollout to a new kernel selection heuristic in the US‑east pool. Under certain sequence length patterns we saw KV cache thrashing that increased p99 latency and caused downstream timeouts. The rollout has been paused; we rolled a targeted rollback at 04:10 UTC this morning which stabilized the tail.

Measured impact (prelim windows aggregated):
- Total request volume in window: ~1.2M requests
- Requests with latency >1s or 5xx: 198,450 (~16.5%)
- Effective error/timeouts causing retries: ~73,000 requests (6% of total window)

Ops recommendation for a concession: a two‑part, tiered remedy — aligned to Mateo’s framing but with explicit caps and a goodwill voucher:
1) Retroactive invoice credit for July billed amount based on measured affected percentage (rounded down to nearest 5% band):
   - <=5% affected: 5% credit
   - >5% to <=15%: 15% credit
   - >15% to <=25%: 25% credit
   - >25%: 40% credit
2) One‑time goodwill voucher for platform credits or dedicated burst capacity: $25,000 cap (to be used within 90 days)
3) Total company exposure capped at $75,000 for this incident (sum of credit + voucher).

Rationale: this aligns financial exposure with measured customer impact and gives NovaHealth immediate runway while we validate fixes. We will deliver a detailed CSV of impacted request ids and timestamps (ops_impact_notes.txt) to NovaHealth under their NDA.

Requesting Marissa and Jonas for executive approval on the proposed cap/credit bands. If approved, I’ll prepare the ledger calc and a DocuSign amendment.

— Karthik Iyer
Director, Production Reliability
Redwood Inference

Attachments: ops_impact_notes.txt (txt)

On Mon, Jun 28, 2026 at 11:05 AM Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Olivia — thank you for flagging this and sorry for the disruption. I pulled the prelim signals and have looped in Karthik and Marissa to get ops and commercial aligned.
> 
> Early proposal (subject to ops validation): a tiered retroactive offset that scales with measured impact...


From: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Olivia Grant <olivia@novahealth.com>
Date: Tue, 30 Jun 2026 16:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: API latency spikes impacting NovaHealth production

Thanks team — I reviewed Karthik’s impact numbers and the proposed bands. I approve the following one‑off remediation package for NovaHealth, with the conditions below:

Approved package (one incident only):
- Retroactive invoice credit: 25% credit applied to NovaHealth’s July invoice (aligned to the >15% to <=25% band)
- One‑time goodwill voucher: $25,000 credit usable for hosted consumption or Dedicated burst capacity (expires 90 days from issuance)
- Company exposure cap: remain at $75,000 (we will not exceed this for the incident)

Conditions and next steps:
1) Legal to prepare a short amendment and DocuSign (Marissa — please coordinate). The amendment will: note this is a one‑time, non‑precedent concession and requires acceptance by NovaHealth within 10 business days.
2) Finance to record a contra‑revenue line for the credit period and tag this under deal D-3479 (Marissa/Finance).
3) Ops to provide the final verified impact CSV and post‑mortem (Karthik) by 7/6/2026.
4) Mateo — please send a cordial note to Olivia confirming the approved package and attach the DocuSign link once ready. Keep tone clear that this is remedial and not a change to our SLOs.

I’m signing off on this to keep the account stable — good work moving quickly.

— Jonas Weber
VP, Revenue Operations
Redwood Inference

From: Olivia Hart <olivia@zephyrevents.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 12 Jun 2026 10:15:00 -0700
Subject: Unconference sponsorship package + spotlight option

Hi Mateo,

Thanks for the quick intro call yesterday — great to hear Redwood is interested in the Unconference format. I attached our latest sponsorship package (Sponsorship_Package_v3.pdf) with a new "Spotlight Roundtable" option we piloted last quarter.

Quick highlights:
- 75-minute moderated roundtable (limited to 40 attendees) in a branded breakout room
- Mobile app spotlight card for 48 hours and a push notification on day-of
- Single 10-minute stage intro (not a full keynote) + dedicated booth hours
- Logo on lanyards and mobile app; tiered placement in printed materials

Our usual keynote slot is included in the top-tier only, but we can discuss trading a keynote for an expanded Spotlight package if that gives you better attendee access. Fee schedule and AV specs are in the PDF.

Are you able to confirm interest in the roundtable trade, and if so what budget band you’re in? I can pull a redline SOW after we lock scope.

Best,

title: SIG: Request for extended inference-trace retention exception (NimbusAI)
preview:
From: Julia Park <julia.park@nimbus.ai>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.inference>
Cc: legal@nimbus.ai
Date: Sat, 20 Jun 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Request: extended retention window for inference traces (SIG)

Hi Mateo,

Thanks again for the product demo last week — excited to move forward. Our security team is completing the SIG/CAIQ review and has one open ask we need to triage before procurement: we require a 180-day retention window for inference traces (request/response + debug traces) for a subset of tenants used in regulated workflows.

Specifically: can Redwood make/approve a per-tenant retention exception for \"inference-trace\" logs to be retained for 180 days instead of the standard 30 days? Nimbus is prepared to sign a limited-scoped addendum and accept additional controls (keystore residency constraints, encrypted-at-rest attestation, and a quarterly evidence bundle).

Attached: nimbus_SIG_questions.pdf (annotated SIG with checkboxes)

Questions:
- Is a per-tenant retention variance technically supportable in Hosted mode?
- What compensating controls would Redwood require?
- What evidence artifacts (exports, deletion logs) can you provide on a quarterly cadence?

Can you please loop in security and let us know the feasibility and an evidence plan by EOD Monday? This is on the critical path for our SSO+PSC procurement.

Best,
Julia Park
Head of Security Programs
Nimbus AI



From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.inference>
To: Julia Park <julia.park@nimbus.ai>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.inference>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.inference>
Date: Sat, 20 Jun 2026 10:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: extended retention window for inference traces (SIG)

Hi Julia — thanks for the clear ask and for the annotated SIG. I’ve looped in Kimberly (security) and Naomi (customer success) to scope feasibility and evidence. Quick reply with next steps:

- Kimberly: can you confirm whether our Hosted offering can toggle tenant-level retention for the inference-trace index and what controls we’d require (e.g., dedicated AES-256 keys, keystore residency, role-based erase authorization)?
- Naomi: I’ll need help documenting the quarterly evidence bundle and the change to the contract (tenant-scoped addendum).

Julia: we’ll aim to get you a proposed control set and evidence list by Monday EOD. If security requires a short workshop to go over keystore residency, we can set one up early next week.

Mateo Alvarez
Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
mateo.alvarez@redwood.inference
(415) 555-0193



From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.inference>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.inference>
Cc: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.inference>, Julia Park <julia.park@nimbus.ai>
Date: Mon, 22 Jun 2026 08:34:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: extended retention window for inference traces (SIG)

Hi all — reviewed the annotated SIG and the ask. Summary of feasibility and proposed compensating controls (high level):

1) Feasibility
- Tenant-level retention override for the \"inference-trace\" index is technically possible in Hosted (we partition customer telemetry by tenant id). We can apply a TTL policy at index-level per tenant and maintain separate retention buckets.
- Impact: modest additional storage and snapshot costs; will add a per-tenant configuration flag and a guardrail that requires security sign-off before enabling.

2) Required/Recommended Compensating Controls
- Keystore residency: customer key in KMS/HSM with a documented key policy. For Nimbus we recommend KMS key with restricted roles and separate rotation schedule.
- Access controls: strict RBAC for log access; create a dedicated read-only role for your auditors that can only access the 180-day tenant bucket and only via signed API tokens.
- Delete workflow: when the exception expires or is revoked, we will run a verified delete job and provide a signed deletion manifest.
- Monitoring & alerts: attach an audit trace for any export or deletion operation, preserved for 2 years.

3) Evidence artifacts (quarterly bundle) — we can provide the following artifacts as PDFs/CSV:
- Retention configuration export (tenant id, retention window, enable date)
- Signed deletion manifests (hashes + timestamps) for any deletions performed in the quarter
- Keystore policy snapshot and key rotation log (redacted where appropriate)
- Export access logs (who/when) for the 180-day bucket
- A short test deletion report (one sample tenant) with verification hashes

4) Controls we cannot support in Hosted without Dedicated/private deployment
- Full customer-managed HSM inside the same tenancy (we can integrate with external KMS but not host a customer HSM in our multi-tenant Hosted plane).

Attachments: keystore-rotation-memo.pdf (high-level recommended settings)

Next steps: Naomi — can you put together the addendum language template and a list of requested fields for the quarterly bundle? Julia — if this controls list looks acceptable, we can schedule a 30-min tech review and provide sample artifact exports within 48 hours.

Thanks,
Kimberly Park
Head of Security & Trust
Redwood Inference
kimberly.park@redwood.inference



From: Julia Park <julia.park@nimbus.ai>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.inference>, Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.inference>
Cc: legal@nimbus.ai
Date: Mon, 22 Jun 2026 15:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: extended retention window for inference traces (SIG)

Thanks, Kimberly — that scope looks reasonable and aligns with Nimbus' risk model. A few minor clarifications from Legal:

- We will sign a 180-day tenant-scoped addendum tied to the deal id (DEAL-7892).
- Legal requests the deletion manifest include SHA256 hashes and the identity of the runner (service account email) for auditability.
- For keystore residency, we will accept a KMS integration (customer-managed key) with documented rotation policy; HSM is not required.

Please send the proposed addendum language and a first sample evidence bundle (redacted) by Wednesday EOD so our procurement can close. If you can provide a short recording (10–15min) of the export + delete verification flow, that would be great for our auditors.

Julia Park
Head of Security Programs
Nimbus AI

title: Pilot qualification: zero-ttl & short-log options for BlueCart
preview:
From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com>
To: Amelia Torres <amelia@bluecart.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 15 Jun 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Pilot qualification: zero-ttl & short-log options for BlueCart

Hi Amelia / BlueCart team — great to meet earlier. Quick recap from our discovery call + proposed next steps so we can align on a pilot and procurement ask.

TL;DR: BlueCart asked for \"no retention\" of PII and a short log window for non-PII request traces. We need to confirm what you classify as PII (customer names, emails, payment tokens?) and whether you require 0-retention for model input, logs, or both.

Summary of what we captured:
- Use case: customer support triage + automated order summary generation (live traffic ~700 req/day, spikes to 2k).
- Data classes: product SKUs, order IDs, customer name, email, partial card digits. Amelia said anything that can identify a customer should be treated as PII.
- Requirement from BlueCart: zero-ttl for PII; 72-hour or less logs for non-PII; evidence for KMS/SOC2; contractual DPA.

What we can offer today (proposed):
- Hosted API: standard logs + 30-day retention (default). We can provide SOC2 evidence and DPA. Pseudo-anonymization options available but not certified as 0-ttl for raw inputs.
- Dedicated/Private deployment: can support stricter retention, including technically enforced 0-ttl for inputs if customer-managed keys and private deployment are used. Requires an enterprise commit.
- Short-term compromise: ephemeral logs (3–7 days) + explicit PII redaction/pseudo-anonymization step in the ingestion path for hosted pilot.

Attachments: SOC2-questionnaire.pdf (attached), retention-options.csv (rough matrix of options).

Next steps I propose:
1) Amelia: confirm which fields you consider PII and whether 0-ttl must apply to model inputs, inference logs, or both.
2) If BlueCart can run pilot in hosted mode: we test with an ingestion redactor and 3-day short logs. If not acceptable, discuss dedicated/private.
3) Loop procurement (we have a HubSpot deal linked) and pick a 30–45 minute call to lock scope.

Thanks — I can be flexible on timing this week for a short follow-up.

Best,
Mateo Alvarez
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
mateo.alvarez@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0147

Attachment(s): SOC2-questionnaire.pdf, retention-options.csv


From: Amelia Torres <amelia@bluecart.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com>
Cc: Liam Chen <liam.chen@bluecart.com>, procurement@bluecart.com
Date: Tue, 16 Jun 2026 11:03:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Pilot qualification: zero-ttl & short-log options for BlueCart

Hi Mateo — thanks for the clear recap. Quick answers:
- PII for us = full name, email address, postal address, phone, full payment instrument (we can provide a named field list). Order IDs are okay to retain if pseudonymized.
- Zero-ttl requirement: must be for any raw PII appearing in requests/inputs. We understand this is a high bar; for pilot we can be flexible if you can show a technical design that ensures PII is never stored AND provide SOC2 evidence plus a signed DPA.
- Logs: target is 72h for request/response traces (non-PII).

Procurement (procurement@bluecart.com) will want to see a DPA and the SOC2 questionnaire back. Liam will coordinate timing for an internal security review next Thursday.

Can you outline the technical options for zero-ttl? If Redwood needs a dedicated/private environment to ensure zero-ttl for PII we need that spelled out.

Best,
Amelia Torres
Head of Platform Security | BlueCart Inc.
amelia@bluecart.com


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Amelia Torres <amelia@bluecart.com>, Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 17 Jun 2026 08:27:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pilot qualification: zero-ttl & short-log options for BlueCart

Amelia — quick note from product/security side on options and constraints.

Technical options we can present for pilot
1) Hosted + redaction pipeline (fastest go-live): requests pass through a pre-ingest redactor that removes or tokenizes PII before it hits our model runtime. Redactor lives in the customer integration layer (customer-managed or ours). Logs retained for 72 hours (can be reduced to 48 in pilot). This does not give \"true\" 0-ttl for the original raw input unless the redactor is customer-managed and raw payloads are never sent.

2) Hosted + ephemeral store + HSM-wrapped keys: we can store inputs encrypted with customer-supplied KMS and schedule automatic deletion within 24–72 hours. Requires DPA; still not the same as never storing plaintext.

3) Private/Dedicated: for customers willing to take a private deployment (VPC or on-prem), we can configure the runtime not to persist request payloads and rotate keys under customer control. This is the path to satisfy a strict 0-ttl commitment for PII. Enterprise contract required.

Questions for BlueCart to confirm
- Will you run the redactor on your side (preferred) or do you want Redwood to operate it?
- Is 48–72h acceptable as an interim log window if we combine it with PII redaction?

We will attach a short matrix of tradeoffs (retention-options.csv) and the SOC2 questionnaire compiled.

—Marissa
Director, Product — Security & Compliance | Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com


From: Liam Chen <liam.chen@bluecart.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Amelia Torres <amelia@bluecart.com>, procurement@bluecart.com
Date: Thu, 18 Jun 2026 10:46:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Pilot qualification: zero-ttl & short-log options for BlueCart

Thanks — this is helpful. A few clarifications from our end before we pull procurement in for commercials:
- BlueCart can run its own redactor in front of Redwood for the pilot (preferred). We prefer to avoid sending plaintext PII to vendors.

From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com>

Subject: Pilot qualification: zero-ttl & short-log options for BlueCart

Hi Amelia / BlueCart team — great to meet earlier. Quick recap from our discovery call + proposed next steps so we can align on a pilot and procurement ask.

TL;DR: BlueCart asked for \"no retention\" of PII and a short log window for non-PII request traces. We need to confirm what you classify as PII (customer names, emails, payment tokens?) and whether you require 0-retention for model input, logs, or both.

Summary of what we captured:
- Use case: customer support triage + automated order summary generation (live traffic ~700 req/day, spikes to 2k).
- Data classes: product SKUs, order IDs, customer name, email, partial card digits. Amelia said anything that can identify a customer should be treated as PII.
- Requirement from BlueCart: zero-ttl for PII; 72-hour or less logs for non-PII; evidence for KMS/SOC2; contractual DPA.

What we can offer today (proposed):
- Hosted API: standard logs + 30-day retention (default). We can provide SOC2 evidence and DPA. Pseudo-anonymization options available but not certified as 0-ttl for raw inputs.
- Dedicated/Private deployment: can support stricter retention, including technically enforced 0-ttl for inputs if customer-managed keys and private deployment are used. Requires an enterprise commit.
- Short-term compromise: ephemeral logs (3–7 days) + explicit PII redaction/pseudo-anonymization step in the ingestion path for hosted pilot.

Attachments: SOC2-questionnaire.pdf (attached), retention-options.csv (rough matrix of options).

Next steps I propose:
1) Amelia: confirm which fields you consider PII and whether 0-ttl must apply to model inputs, inference logs, or both.
2) If BlueCart can run pilot in hosted mode: we test with an ingestion redactor and 3-day short logs. If not acceptable, discuss dedicated/private.
3) Loop procurement (we have a HubSpot deal linked) and pick a 30–45 minute call to lock scope.

Thanks — I can be flexible on timing this week for a short follow-up.

Best,
Mateo Alvarez
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
mateo.alvarez@redwood.com

Subject: Re: Pilot qualification: zero-ttl & short-log options for BlueCart

Hi Mateo — thanks for the clear recap. Quick answers:
- PII for us = full name, email address, postal address, phone, full payment instrument (we can provide a named field list). Order IDs are okay to retain if pseudonymized.
- Zero-ttl requirement: must be for any raw PII appearing in requests/inputs. We understand this is a high bar; for pilot we can be flexible if you can show a technical design that ensures PII is never stored AND provide SOC2 evidence plus a signed DPA.
- Logs: target is 72h for request/response traces (non-PII).

Procurement (procurement@bluecart.com) will want to see a DPA and the SOC2 questionnaire back. Liam will coordinate timing for an internal security review next Thursday.

Can you outline the technical options for zero-ttl? If Redwood needs a dedicated/private environment to ensure zero-ttl for PII we need that spelled out.

Best,
Amelia Torres
Head of Platform Security | BlueCart Inc.
amelia@bluecart.com


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Amelia Torres <amelia@bluecart.com>, Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 17 Jun 2026 08:27:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pilot qualification: zero-ttl & short-log options for BlueCart

Amelia — quick note from product/security side on options and constraints.

Technical options we can present for pilot
1) Hosted + redaction pipeline (fastest go-live): requests pass through a pre-ingest redactor that removes or tokenizes PII before it hits our model runtime. Redactor lives in the customer integration layer (customer-managed or ours). Logs retained for 72 hours (can be reduced to 48 in pilot). This does not give \"true\" 0-ttl for the original raw input unless the redactor is customer-managed and raw payloads are never sent.

2) Hosted + ephemeral store + HSM-wrapped keys: we can store inputs encrypted with customer-supplied KMS and schedule automatic deletion within 24–72 hours. Requires DPA; still not the same as never storing plaintext.

3) Private/Dedicated: for customers willing to take a private deployment (VPC or on-prem), we can configure the runtime not to persist request payloads and rotate keys under customer control. This is the path to satisfy a strict 0-ttl commitment for PII. Enterprise contract required.

Questions for BlueCart to confirm
- Will you run the redactor on your side (preferred) or do you want Redwood to operate it?
- Is 48–72h acceptable as an interim log window if we combine it with PII redaction?

We will attach a short matrix of tradeoffs (retention-options.csv) and the SOC2 questionnaire compiled.

—Marissa
Director, Product — Security & Compliance | Redwood Inference

Hi Logan,

Thanks again for the demo last week. We're ready to get HealthCore into your procurement portal (Ariba) but we've run into two blockers on our side that I wanted to raise early: 

1) The MSA we returned has a handful of redlines (attached) where our legal team requires a 30-day remedy window for outages and a more prescriptive data-handling section. Your order form in the portal currently references Redwood's standard SLA and data appendix which do not line up with our redlines. Can you confirm how you handle MSA redline exceptions when registration is initiated through Ariba?

2) Ariba mapping: our Proc Ops team is seeing that Redwood's portal expects a PO acknowledgement flag (PO_ACK_REQUIRED) and an invoice remit field that doesn't map cleanly from our PO feed. If that field is required to progress activation, we'll likely need a temporary exception or a field-mapping workaround.

I've looped in our procurement mailbox (procureops@healthcore.com) for any necessary file transfers. Attached: HealthCore_MSA_redline_v3.pdf and a short checklist of required fields.

Can you advise next steps and whether we should pause registration until legal signs off, or proceed with a provisioning hold while legal finishes review?

Best,
Susan Barlow
Sr. Procurement Manager
HealthCore Systems
Attachments: HealthCore_MSA_redline_v3.pdf, Ariba_registration_checklist.xlsx

Subject: Re: HealthCore onboarding — portal registration + MSA redline

Susan — thanks for the heads up and for sending the redlines. Appreciate the clarity.

Quick summary of how Redwood typically handles this: for portal-driven onboarding we can do one of two flows — (A) gate activation until legal executes an MSA (preferred for enterprise customers with non-standard terms), or (B) allow registration to proceed but hold provisioning (no model keys issued) until countersignature. We can also accept temporary SLA exceptions on a per-deal basis if approved by legal and the contracts team.

Action items I'm proposing:
- I attached the redline you sent and flagged the clauses that conflict with our standard portal order form.
- Kimberly, can you confirm whether the 30-day remedy window is something we can accept (and if so, any suggested language)?
- Marissa, can ops stand up a temporary mapping that writes HealthCore's PO_ACK to our PO_ACK_REQUIRED flag so registration doesn't fail? If not, what's the minimum gating field set?
- Mateo, help coordinate the HubSpot/DocuSign record so we can track the legal exception approval.

If we want to move faster, we can provision a staging account (limited throughput) while legal runs the redline. That keeps testing live but prevents production invoicing.

Susan — thanks for the heads up and for sending the redlines. Appreciate the clarity.

Quick summary of how Redwood typically handles this: for portal-driven onboarding we can do one of two flows — (A) gate activation until legal executes an MSA (preferred for enterprise customers with non-standard terms), or (B) allow registration to proceed but hold provisioning (no model keys issued) until countersignature. We can also accept temporary SLA exceptions on a per-deal basis if approved by legal and the contracts team.

Action items I'm proposing:
- I attached the redline you sent and flagged the clauses that conflict with our standard portal order form.
- Kimberly, can you confirm whether the 30-day remedy window is something we can accept (and if so, any suggested language)?
- Marissa, can ops stand up a temporary mapping that writes HealthCore's PO_ACK to our PO_ACK_REQUIRED flag so registration doesn't fail? If not, what's the minimum gating field set?
- Mateo, help coordinate the HubSpot/DocuSign record so we can track the legal exception approval.

If we want to move faster, we can provision a staging account (limited throughput) while legal runs the redline. That keeps testing live but prevents production invoicing.

title: Outbound authorization and stewardship for private endpoint integrations
preview:
From: Ops Team <ops@forsythhealth.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo@redwood.example.com>
Date: Mon, 15 Jun 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Private endpoint outbound: binding & proof required

Hi Mateo,

We're getting close to finalizing network changes for the private deployment of Redwood at Forsyth. Network security has two items they need from the vendor before we close:

1) Definitive list of egress IP addresses (or ranges) we can allowlist on our perimeter for the control plane and private runtime callouts. If any of these addresses are ephemeral or rotated, we need the rotation window and a fallback policy.

2) A short description of recommended firewall rules (ports, FQDN/SNI behaviour, TLS inspection impact) and whether Redwood can support an outbound proxy (customer-terminating mTLS) instead of static IPs.

3) Evidence pack for audit: attestations or a short PDF stating the model by which outbound IPs are assigned, any autoscaling IP ranges, and the team contact for escalations.

Can you point us to the authoritative artifact and who on your side will own the stewarding of outbound addresses?

Thanks,
Ben Atwell
Network Ops Manager
Forsyth Health

--
Attachment: schema of our firewall (referenced in ticket)


From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo@redwood.example.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.example.com>, David Kim <david@redwood.example.com>
Cc: Soojin Lee <soojin@redwood.example.com>
Date: Mon, 15 Jun 2026 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Private endpoint outbound: binding & proof required

Forwarding Ben's ask from Forsyth. TL;DR: they need a formal statement of how we present egress addresses for private deployments, whether we can support a customer-provided proxy+mtls option, and an evidence pack for audit.

Please reply with an owner and recommended approach. Quick decisions we need:
- Do we provide a small fixed CIDR block per customer for control-plane egress?
- For private runtime egress, do we require customer NAT (recommended) or can we optionally provide a short-lived static egress pool with rotation windows?
- What artifacts can we attach that satisfy an auditor (attestation PDF + IP lease table + config snippets)?

I'm happy to loop Ben back once you propose a one-page approach and accompanying artefacts.

Thanks,
Mateo
Sr. Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.example.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo@redwood.example.com>, David Kim <david@redwood.example.com>
Cc: Soojin Lee <soojin@redwood.example.com>
Date: Tue, 16 Jun 2026 14:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Private endpoint outbound: binding & proof required

Mateo / team — here's a concise, operationally-safe recommendation we can send to Forsyth. I attach two small artifacts: (1) egress-ip-delegation.pdf (one-page policy + ownership); (2) nexus-proxy-config-snippet.txt (proxy snippet showing mTLS server cert expectation and SNI passthrough).

Recommendations (short):

1) Control plane / management traffic (agents / health checks / config pull): provide a stable, small set of static IPs we own (two /32s for redundancy) that are reserved per-region. These are long-lived and used for management calls only. Acceptable firewall rule: destination IPs = {A.B.C.80, A.B.C.81}, destination port 443, TLS with SNI preserved (no TLS interception required).

2) Private runtime egress (customer inference / model fetching): preferred model is customer-provided NAT IPs (customer-controlled outbound), i.e., their own managed NAT addressing. This keeps egress ownership with the customer and simplifies audits. If customer cannot provide NAT, we can offer a managed egress pool with the following constraints:
   - Assigned ephemeral pool: a /29 allocated for the tenant for the duration of the contract; nightly rotation window documented (e.g., rotation at 03:00 UTC weekly) OR predictable lease durations (30 days) depending on compliance needs.
   - For rotation: we provide a signed IP-rotation log and 48-hour warm/fallback window where previous IPs continue to accept traffic to avoid connection breakage.

3) Proxy option (customer chooses): support inbound mTLS from our runtime to a customer-controlled proxy endpoint. In that case, we only require outbound connectivity to the customer's proxy FQDN (DNS allowlist) and the certificate-backed mTLS handshake. This is best where IP allowlist is impossible. Note: DNS allowlist + SNI is acceptable only if the customer allows TLS passthrough and does not perform TLS interception.

4) Firewall guidance: allow HTTPS (443) to the listed IPs/FQDNs; optionally whitelist SNI values if deep inspection is present. Avoid broad CIDR allowances — prefer single-host /32 or per-tenant /29 where managed. Document ephemeral rotation windows and sample flow logs for the auditor.

Artifacts to attach to for audit:
- egress-ip-delegation.pdf (who owns IPs, lease windows, rotation policy)
- attestation-checklist.xlsx (fields: IP, lease_start, lease_end, owner_contact, rotation_policy)
- nexus-proxy-config-snippet.txt (shows mTLS client expectations and TTL for keepalive)

Action items I recommend we send to Ben in the next email:
- Confirm whether Forsyth will provide NAT egress. If yes -> provide them the control-plane static IPs and the proxy snippet only.
- If they cannot provide NAT -> propose a /29 managed allocation plus the attestation checklist and sign-off owner (we will need Legal/Finance to accept the IP lease terms).

I'll volunteer to be the internal steward for the attestation artifacts and to shepherd the DocuSign if Legal needs it. David, can you confirm feasibility of a per-tenant /29 and the rotation logging? Soojin, please confirm any telemetry we can produce (flow logs / connection samples) to populate the attestation checklist.

— Marissa
Director, Technical Solutions
Redwood Inference

Attachments referenced:
- egress-ip-delegation.pdf (attached)
- nexus-proxy-config-snippet.txt (attached)
- attestation-checklist.xlsx (attached)


From: Ben Atwell <ben@northbridge-ops.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo@redwood.example.com>
Cc: integrations@forsyth-health.org
Date: Wed, 17 Jun 2026 08:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Outbound authorization and stewardship for private endpoint integrations

Mateo / Marissa — thanks for the clear options and the attachments. Forsyth security reviewed the suggested approaches and prefers the mTLS proxy option (customer-terminating), but they asked for the following before we schedule the final sign-off call:

- A one-page attestation PDF that states Redwood's managed egress model when the customer requests a managed pool (include rotation windows and the 48-hour warm period).
- Confirmation that the control-plane IPs are stable for 12 months (we will allowlist them now; ops will expect those to remain unchanged unless we are explicitly notified 60 days in advance).
- A simple set of firewall snippets we can drop into our perimeter (CIDR or FQDN + port + sample SNI pattern).

If you can provide the one-page attestation and the firewall snippet by EOD tomorrow we can move to the DocuSign step. Appreciate the fast turn here.

Best,
Ben
Network Ops Manager, Forsyth Health


(thread ends)

- Confirmation that the control-plane IPs are stable for 12 months (we will allowlist them now; ops will expect those to remain unchanged unless we are explicitly notified 60 days in advance).
- A simple set of firewall snippets we can drop into our perimeter (CIDR or FQDN + port + sample SNI pattern).

If you can provide the one-page attestation and the firewall snippet by EOD tomorrow we can move to the DocuSign step. Appreciate the fast turn here.

Best,
Ben
Network Ops Manager, Forsyth Health


(thread ends)

title: Private deployment: launch readiness sweep / account & network validation
preview:
From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo_alvarez@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Elder <samantha.elder@acmehealth.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>, Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 10 Jun 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Private deployment: launch readiness sweep / account & network validation

Samantha / Acme Cloud Ops team,

Thanks again for choosing Redwood Private. To make the handoff to your infra team as smooth as possible, I'd like to run a short launch readiness sweep this month focused on account setup, network validation, and environment requirements. I attached a working checklist to this thread (redwood-vpc-launch-checklist.xlsx) and included the high-level plan below.

High-level sweep (target timeline: next 7 business days):
- Account & IAM: verify RW service account, KMS/HSM access, and CI/service principal permissions.
- Networking: confirm VPC peering / private endpoints, firewall rules, NAT/GW egress rules, and MTU/DF settings.
- Environment: node sizing, GPU allocation, swap/hugepages/transparent_hugepage settings, disk provisioning and IOPS baseline.
- Security/compliance: syslog/s3 audit export, control-plane connectivity tests, and SOC2 evidence locations.

Requests for your engineering team: please confirm the following before we schedule validation windows:
1) The cloud account and billing owner email for Redwood onboarding (if different from contract).
2) Contact for network engineer (for peering, subnets, and firewall coordination).
3) Preferred dates/times for a 90-minute validation window where we can run connectivity & perf checks (we'll need ports opened temporarily for control-plane handshakes).

Notes/attachments: redwood-vpc-launch-checklist.xlsx (draft), acme-network-segmentation-202606.pdf (we referenced in the SOW).

Best,
Mateo Alvarez
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
mateo_alvarez@redwood.ai
+1 415 555 0198

--
(referenced checklist attached)

To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo_alvarez@redwood.ai>

- For the validation window, next week Tue/Wed mornings (EST) look best; can you do Tue 2026-06-16 10:00-11:30 EST?

- Our perimeter firewall requires explicit outbound port ranges allowed for the control-plane (we'll add those once you confirm).
- We're running secondary routing via a transit gateway; we need to confirm that Redwood's peering flow handles path MTU <= 1500.

I'll get the finance owner included if we need to do any billing account-level changes. Also attaching our internal network segmentation doc (acme-network-segmentation-202606.pdf) for reference — it's the same doc you have but with internal VLAN IDs redacted.

title: Ingress conduit decision: direct peering, service endpoint, or encrypted tunnel for Private LLM stack
preview:
From: Alice Davenport <alice.davenport@acme-analytics.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo@redwood.ai>
Cc: netops@acme-analytics.com
Date: Wed, 10 Jun 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Private deployment connectivity — quick background and asks

Hi Mateo,

Thanks for taking this. We're evaluating Redwood Private for a pilot (in our us-west account). NetOps attached the requirements workbook but high level: low-latency inference for customer-facing flows (99th pct latency target <= 120ms), strict audit logging, and traffic must remain within our account boundary unless otherwise approved.

Three connectivity options were suggested by our infra team and we'd like Redwood's guidance on tradeoffs and an ops plan for each:
- Direct VPC peering between our VPC and Redwood's private VPC
- AWS PrivateLink (interface endpoints) to Redwood's service endpoints
- Site-to-site IPsec VPN back to Redwood-managed gateway (or your recommended tunnel)

Can you summarize recommended default + fallback, expected latency implications, auth requirements, and any compliance caveats? Also please include a short mutual action plan (M.A.P.) we can attach to procurement.

We've attached Acme_network_requirements.xlsx with traffic patterns and regulatory notes.

Cheers,
Alice
Alice Davenport
Head of Platform Engineering — Acme Analytics
alice.davenport@acme-analytics.com

[attachment: Acme_network_requirements.xlsx (XLSX)]

From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo@redwood.ai>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.ai>, Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>
Cc: Alice Davenport <alice.davenport@acme-analytics.com>, netops@acme-analytics.com
Date: Wed, 10 Jun 2026 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Private deployment connectivity — quick background and asks

Team — forwarding Acme's ask. Short answer up-front: PrivateLink as primary for tenant isolation and least blast radius; peering for simplest L3 path when both accounts can accept route visibility; S2S VPN only as fallback for cross-account constraints or temporary workarounds. That said, each customer profile changes the recommendation.

Can Kimberly and Irene take the lead on a concise decision matrix and a one-page ops checklist? Marcus, please run a rough latency sensitivity note (we have Acme's 99p <= 120ms). I put initial notes inlined below. I'll merge into a MAP to send to Acme.

Initial bullets:
- PrivateLink: strong isolation (interface endpoints), minimal route exposure, easier to satisfy audit controls. Adds per-ENI and data-processing hops — may add ~8–30ms depending on region and NLB placement.
- VPC peering: single-hop L3 routing, typically lower packet processing latency, but requires cross-account route management and increases blast radius for L3-level attacks/misconfigurations. Not ideal when customers require strict control of inbound endpoints.
- S2S VPN: encrypted tunnel, relatively easy if customer wants to keep everything in their VPC boundary, but potential variability in latency and throughput; long-term ops cost higher (IPsec management, BGP flaps).

Attachments: saving a skeleton constraints file for the matrix.

-Mateo
Mateo Alvarez
Solutions Architect — Redwood Inference
mateo@redwood.ai
[attachment: initial-constraints.txt (txt)]

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.ai>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo@redwood.ai>, Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>
Cc: Alice Davenport <alice.davenport@acme-analytics.com>, netops@acme-analytics.com
Date: Thu, 11 Jun 2026 09:18:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Private deployment connectivity — quick background and asks

All — I pulled together a short matrix and an ops checklist. See summary below (full matrix attached as connectivity-matrix.csv and a short latency sim as latency-sla-sim.pdf).

Summary matrix (high level):

1) VPC Peering
- Pros: Lowest hop-count once established; predictable intra-cloud bandwidth; no per-connection data processing charges.
- Cons: Requires route propagation across peered VPCs (cross-account). Increases surface area: any misconfigured security group or route can expose services. Not supported across regions without additional hops. Maintenance: changes to CIDR and overlapping ranges create outages risk.
- Expected latency impact: typically +3–10ms inside same AZ; cross-AZ adds a small variance. For Acme's 99p <=120ms target, peering typically gives the best headroom.

2) AWS PrivateLink (Interface Endpoints + NLB on Redwood side)
- Pros: Strong tenant isolation, no route exposure in customer VPC (traffic to endpoint stays within customer VPC until egress to the endpoint ENI). Simplifies compliance (we can provide endpoint-level audit evidence). Support for cross-account access via endpoint policies.
- Cons: Adds extra ENI hops and depending on NLB placement, can add 8–30ms median additional latency. Also per-GB data processing and endpoint hourly costs — need to model heavy inference traffic. For bursty short requests, cost delta can be material.
- Operational notes: We recommend colocating Redwood NLBs in customer region and enabling IPv6 dual-stack if needed. Endpoint policies must be explicit.

3) Site-to-site IPsec VPN
- Pros: Customer keeps full VPC control; easy when customer has strict peering policies. Good for temporary or low-throughput pilots.
- Cons: Latency variability, throughput limits on managed VPN/edge appliances, higher ops overhead. IPsec tunnels require monitoring and rotation of pre-shared keys or better—BGP + IKEv2 recommended.
- Expected latency: depends on tunnel path and encryption overhead — could add 20–100+ ms if routing detours occur. Not ideal for sub-120ms 99p unless carefully engineered with dedicated appliances and high-quality transit.

Operational recommendation (for Acme):
- Preferred: PrivateLink by default where compliance wants minimal route visibility and we can accept the incremental per-request latency. We provide a hardened endpoint policy, per-endpoint audit logs, and an ops runbook.
- Performance-sensitive flows (strict sub-120ms 99p): evaluate peering for those specific VPC pairs or a colocated edge NLB to reduce backend hop latency. This is a hybrid: PrivateLink for control plane and non-latency-critical traffic, peering for the hot inference paths.
- Fallback: S2S VPN only for short-term trials or if Acme's procurement disallows cross-account endpoints.

Checklist highlights (ops):
- CIDR and route validation: confirm no overlaps; propose transient NAT for overlap cases.
- Endpoint policies: least-privilege and tied to service principals.
- Monitoring: enable VPC flow logs + endpoint flow logging; collect 99/95/50p latency metrics for first 2 weeks.
- IAM: service-account roles for tunnel config and endpoint admin; KMS for any shared keys.
- Audit artifacts: provide per-endpoint connection logs and API access logs for SOC evidence.

Attachments: connectivity-matrix.csv, latency-sla-sim.pdf (very small sim based on patterns provided), and a sample MAP draft if Mateo wants to send.

-Kimberly
Kimberly Park
Principal Cloud Engineer — Redwood Inference
kimberly_park@redwood.ai

> On Wed, Jun 10, 2026 at 10:05 AM Mateo Alvarez <mateo@redwood.ai> wrote:
> > Team — forwarding Acme's ask. Short answer up-front: PrivateLink as primary...
> >
> > -Mateo

From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo@redwood.ai>
To: Alice Davenport <alice.davenport@acme-analytics.com>, netops@acme-analytics.com
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.ai>, Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>, CloudLink Systems <support@cloudlink.io>
Date: Fri, 12 Jun 2026 17:45:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Private deployment connectivity — quick background and asks

Alice / Acme NetOps — thanks for the patience. Attached is a concise recommendation and a MAP to move the pilot forward. TL;DR recommendation:

1) Default option: AWS PrivateLink (interface endpoints). Rationale: least blast radius, clean policy boundaries for audit, and simplest consumer-side ops. Redwood will provide a hardened endpoint NLB in us-west-2 and detailed endpoint policies + logs for compliance.

2) Performance mode for hot inference paths: Peer the Acme inference VPC(s) to our dedicated edge VPCs for the pilot only. This hybrid approach keeps control-plane traffic (metrics, admin) via PrivateLink while inference pods use peered routes to shave ~8–15ms median. We will only peer the minimal subnets that host inference egress to limit attack surface.

3) Fallback: S2S IPsec with BGP for temporary onboarding when procurement blocks cross-account endpoints. This is not recommended long-term for latency-sensitive flows.

Next steps / proposed MAP (high level):
- Week 0: Sign mutual NDA (if not already in place) and finalize network CIDRs.
- Week 1: Create PrivateLink interface endpoints in Acme account — Acme to approve endpoint policy; Redwood to provision NLB (us-west-2).
- Week 2: Canary: route a small % of inference traffic through PrivateLink; collect latency/99p metrics.
- Week 3: If hot-path needs tighter latency, provision peering for specified subnets and run a 72-hour soak.
- Week 4: Confirm operational runbook, finalize monitoring dashboards, and cutover.

I've attached: mutual_action_plan.pdf and a short redwood-privatelink-checklist.md that lists the exact API and IAM calls. Kimberly's matrix and latency sim are also attached for your engineering review.

If you want, we can schedule a 30-min hangout with NetOps + our networking SME (Irene) to walk the checklist and endpoint policy. Proposed times next week: Mon/Tue 09:00-11:00 PT.

Thanks — looking forward to the pilot.

-Mateo

From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo@redwood.ai>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.ai>, Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>
Cc: Alice Davenport <alice.davenport@acme-analytics.com>, netops@acme-analytics.com
Date: Wed, 10 Jun 2026 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Private deployment connectivity — quick background and asks

Team — forwarding Acme's ask. Short answer up-front: PrivateLink as primary for tenant isolation and least blast radius; peering for simplest L3 path when both accounts can accept route visibility; S2S VPN only as fallback for cross-account constraints or temporary workarounds. That said, each customer profile changes the recommendation.

Can Kimberly and Irene take the lead on a concise decision matrix and a one-page ops checklist? Marcus, please run a rough latency sensitivity note (we have Acme's 99p <= 120ms). I put initial notes inlined below. I'll merge into a MAP to send to Acme.

Initial bullets:
- PrivateLink: strong isolation (interface endpoints), minimal route exposure, easier to satisfy audit controls. Adds per-ENI and data-processing hops — may add ~8–30ms depending on region and NLB placement.
- VPC peering: single-hop L3 routing, typically lower packet processing latency, but requires cross-account route management and increases blast radius for L3-level attacks/misconfigurations. Not ideal when customers require strict control of inbound endpoints.
- S2S VPN: encrypted tunnel, relatively easy if customer wants to keep everything in their VPC boundary, but potential variability in latency and throughput; long-term ops cost higher (IPsec management, BGP flaps).

Attachments: saving a skeleton constraints file for the matrix.

-Mateo
Mateo Alvarez
Solutions Architect — Redwood Inference
mateo@redwood.ai
[attachment: initial-constraints.txt (txt)]

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.ai>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo@redwood.ai>, Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>
Cc: Alice Davenport <alice.davenport@acme-analytics.com>, netops@acme-analytics.com
Date: Thu, 11 Jun 2026 09:18:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Private deployment connectivity — quick background and asks

All — I pulled together a short matrix and an ops checklist. See summary below (full matrix attached as connectivity-matrix.csv and a short latency sim as latency-sla-sim.pdf).

Summary matrix (high level):

1) VPC Peering
- Pros: Lowest hop-count once established; predictable intra-cloud bandwidth; no per-connection data processing charges.
- Cons: Requires route propagation across peered VPCs (cross-account). Increases surface area: any misconfigured security group or route can expose services. Not supported across regions without additional hops. Maintenance: changes to CIDR and overlapping ranges create outages risk.
- Expected latency impact: typically +3–10ms inside same AZ; cross-AZ adds a small variance. For Acme's 99p <=120ms target, peering typically gives the best headroom.

2) AWS PrivateLink (Interface Endpoints + NLB on Redwood side)
- Pros: Strong tenant isolation, no route exposure in customer VPC (traffic to endpoint stays within customer VPC until egress to the endpoint ENI). Simplifies compliance (we can provide endpoint-level audit evidence). Support for cross-account access via endpoint policies.
- Cons: Adds extra ENI hops and depending on NLB placement, can add 8–30ms median additional latency. Also per-GB data processing and endpoint hourly costs — need to model heavy inference traffic. For bursty short requests, cost delta can be material.
- Operational notes: We recommend colocating Redwood NLBs in customer region and enabling IPv6 dual-stack if needed. Endpoint policies must be explicit.

3) Site-to-site IPsec VPN
- Pros: Customer keeps full VPC control; easy when customer has strict peering policies. Good for temporary or low-throughput pilots.
- Cons: Latency variability, throughput limits on managed VPN/edge appliances, higher ops overhead. IPsec tunnels require monitoring and rotation of pre-shared keys or better—BGP + IKEv2 recommended.
- Expected latency: depends on tunnel path and encryption overhead — could add 20–100+ ms if routing detours occur. Not ideal for sub-120ms 99p unless carefully engineered with dedicated appliances and high-quality transit.

Operational recommendation (for Acme):
- Preferred: PrivateLink by default where compliance wants minimal route visibility and we can accept the incremental per-request latency. We provide a hardened endpoint policy, per-endpoint audit logs, and an ops runbook.
- Performance-sensitive flows (strict sub-120ms 99p): evaluate peering for those specific VPC pairs or a colocated edge NLB to reduce backend hop latency. This is a hybrid: PrivateLink for control plane and non-latency-critical traffic, peering for the hot inference paths.
- Fallback: S2S VPN only for short-term trials or if Acme's procurement disallows cross-account endpoints.

Checklist highlights (ops):
- CIDR and route validation: confirm no overlaps; propose transient NAT for overlap cases.
- Endpoint policies: least-privilege and tied to service principals.
- Monitoring: enable VPC flow logs + endpoint flow logging; collect 99/95/50p latency metrics for first 2 weeks.
- IAM: service-account roles for tunnel config and endpoint admin; KMS for any shared keys.
- Audit artifacts: provide per-endpoint connection logs and API access logs for SOC evidence.

Attachments: connectivity-matrix.csv, latency-sla-sim.pdf (very small sim based on patterns provided), and a sample MAP draft if Mateo wants to send.

-Kimberly
Kimberly Park
Principal Cloud Engineer — Redwood Inference
kimberly_park@redwood.ai

Subject: RE: Private deployment connectivity — quick background and asks

Alice / Acme NetOps — thanks for the patience. Attached is a concise recommendation and a MAP to move the pilot forward. TL;DR recommendation:

1) Default option: AWS PrivateLink (interface endpoints). Rationale: least blast radius, clean policy boundaries for audit, and simplest consumer-side ops. Redwood will provide a hardened endpoint NLB in us-west-2 and detailed endpoint policies + logs for compliance.

2) Performance mode for hot inference paths: Peer the Acme inference VPC(s) to our dedicated edge VPCs for the pilot only. This hybrid approach keeps control-plane traffic (metrics, admin) via PrivateLink while inference pods use peered routes to shave ~8–15ms median. We will only peer the minimal subnets that host inference egress to limit attack surface.

3) Fallback: S2S IPsec with BGP for temporary onboarding when procurement blocks cross-account endpoints. This is not recommended long-term for latency-sensitive flows.

Next steps / proposed MAP (high level):
- Week 0: Sign mutual NDA (if not already in place) and finalize network CIDRs.
- Week 1: Create PrivateLink interface endpoints in Acme account — Acme to approve endpoint policy; Redwood to provision NLB (us-west-2).
- Week 2: Canary: route a small % of inference traffic through PrivateLink; collect latency/99p metrics.
- Week 3: If hot-path needs tighter latency, provision peering for specified subnets and run a 72-hour soak.
- Week 4: Confirm operational runbook, finalize monitoring dashboards, and cutover.

If you want, we can schedule a 30-min hangout with NetOps + our networking SME (Irene) to walk the checklist and endpoint policy. Proposed times next week: Mon/Tue 09:00-11:00 PT.

title: Escrow structure for pilot billing & tax routing
preview:
From: Sarah Chen <sarah.chen@vitalmed.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com>
Cc: Paul Bruner <paul.bruner@vitalmed.com>
Date: 2026-05-28T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Pilot payment cadence & escrow proposal

Hi Mateo —

Thanks again for the walkthrough yesterday. To help us move the pilot forward with the internal procurement and tax teams, VitalMed would like to propose the following billing structure so we can reconcile acceptance milestones and VAT routing across our EU entity: 

- Net60 payment terms for pilot invoices
- Holdback: 20% of each invoice placed into an escrow facility and released upon final milestone acceptance (30 days after acceptance as a practical matter)
- Split: Line items related to data processing and storage to be billed to Redwood EU entity to facilitate VAT reverse-charge; professional services billed to Redwood US entity

I attached the SOW and a rough payment schedule for your reference (VitalMed_SOW_v1.pdf; payment_timeline_vitalmed.xlsx).

Please let us know if Redwood can accept the escrow holdback and split invoicing. Procurement will need a short note on how the escrow will be managed (custodian, release triggers) and confirmation that Redwood can issue a credit note if a milestone is partially rejected.

Happy to hop on a quick call if easier.

Best,
Sarah Chen
Senior Procurement Manager
VitalMed

Attachment stubs: VitalMed_SOW_v1.pdf (doc), payment_timeline_vitalmed.xlsx (spreadsheet)

From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com>
To: Sarah Chen <sarah.chen@vitalmed.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-05-28T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Pilot payment cadence & escrow proposal

Hi Sarah —

Thanks — appreciated detail. Looping in Marcus (finance) and Naomi (AE) to surface cashflow and commercial impacts. Quick initial take from Redwood: 

- Net60 is acceptable for a pilot but we request Net45 as standard for pilots where we provide early capacity. Net45 helps us align monthly close and reduces working capital stress. If Net60 is firm for VitalMed we can explore a modest uplift/credit to offset the longer term.
- Escrow: we can support a holdback, but 20% is higher than our usual practice. Would VitalMed consider 10–15%? Redwood can agree to an escrow custodian (we typically use a neutral agent) and scoped release triggers aligned to the SOW acceptance criteria.
- Invoice split: fine in principle. We'll need the exact legal entity you want billed and the VAT registration numbers for your EU entity to ensure correct treatment.

I attached our updated payment schedule suggestion for a 45-day cadence and a short list of proposed escrow release milestones (Redwood_payment_schedule_v3.pdf).

If Marcus is comfortable with an escrow at 15% and a Net45 cadence, we should be able to get a short contract amendment over to you for signature. Naomi — can you confirm commercial? Marcus — can you confirm funding/cash impact?

Cheers,
Mateo Alvarez
Commercial Lead, Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference

Attachment stub: Redwood_payment_schedule_v3.pdf

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>
Cc: Sarah Chen <sarah.chen@vitalmed.com>, Paul Bruner <paul.bruner@vitalmed.com>
Date: 2026-06-02T10:15:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Pilot payment cadence & escrow proposal

Mateo / Naomi —

Reviewing the ask from procurement: a 20% holdback materially increases our DSO for the pilot. Quick finance notes and a possible compromise: 

- Cash impact: 20% escrow over a 3-month pilot would require ~USD 250–300k incremental working capital based on their expected monthly burn (see attached calc).
- Compromise proposal: 15% escrow, with 50% of the holdback released on partial milestone acceptance (i.e., initial delivery/QA), and the remainder released on final acceptance (or automatically after 30 days if no formal rejection). This reduces Redwood's exposure while preserving VitalMed's leverage for acceptance.
- Conditional short-term credit line: for the pilot only, Redwood can extend a £/€-denominated credit hold for the first invoice cycle (cap at $100k) so there isn't an immediate hit to operations — subject to standard credit checks and a one-time finance admin fee.
- Invoice routing: billing the VAT-applicable lines to Redwood EU entity is workable. We will need VitalMed's EU VAT ID and a signed representation clause in the amendment.

I attached a brief workbook going through the expected monthly cashflows under 10%, 15%, and 20% holdbacks (finance-impact-calcs.xlsx). Naomi — if Sales wants to accept the finance admin fee and the short credit cap, we can greenlight 15% + Net45. Otherwise we should push for Net45 + 10% holdback.

-Marcus
Marcus Lin
Head of Finance, Redwood Inference

Attachment stub: finance-impact-calcs.xlsx
